column8: Get exciting people in front of the camera. There are so many great people who love to tell stories and have so much untold, directors, editors, good colour graders, cameramen... so many treasures to be unearthed.
column9: It frustrates me when the trend is towards excess, superficiality and shallowness. I want to hear masters, understand quality and dive deep. Otherwise, I don't need subscription portals if they're just a copy of Instagram, YouTube or Skillshare.
column10:
Please stay true to quality. 
You can be one of the few platforms with high end that will last a long time. 
The high prices are legitimate. I think community material will quickly become arbitrary.
Look at how Creative Live was destroyed and Masterclass.com went from being high end to being like everyone else, and that's what happened.

https://lowepost.com/ and mzed, on the other hand, are really high-end in comparison and a safe bet. That can still be marketed. The high quality could be even more clearly emphasised. I don't think that inferior quality really brings you anything – it destroys the brand.

column9:
Courses don’t teach the full workflow
Too theoretical, not practical enough
column10: I believe MZed is one of the best filmmaking education platforms, but I also believe it could become truly unmatched by focusing more on practical, budget-friendly lessons for small crews and solo creators, offering complete start-to-finish project walkthroughs, providing clear learning paths from beginner to advanced, and including more hands-on assignments with downloadable resources. A stronger sense of community—such as Q&As or peer feedback—plus more diverse instructors representing documentary, commercial, wedding, and YouTube filmmaking would make the platform even more valuable. Keeping editing and color courses up to date and adding more short, focused micro-lessons would also greatly improve the learning experience.

column8: I currently subscribe, but I am searching for a course on editing that discusses protocol and professional practice rather than platforms. Ex: file naming protocol, handling daiIies, handing off deliverables, etc.  I would also like insight into current set practices (professional, not run and gun DSLR level) in camera assisting, wireless technologies (monitors, focus, etc),operating and data management. I have yet to see any functional courses on focus pulling, image resolution and depth of field (a course I teach at college level but would like to be more informed about). I would love a course in professional assessment of lenses and camera systems (once again, not DSLR level). Content like what Abel Cine used to provide. I have enjoyed the Canon C300 MKII and Arri training videos. I was disappointed in the FX6 video and would like to see something more substantial covering the SONY systems. I was trained on the Venice, and I wish there was something comparable for the FX9 and that series.
column9: See above. Everything I find is prosumer level, or focussed on the flash and not the regular day to day skills required on professional shoots. I can't find deeper dives into cameras, lenses and data management. I am patchworking together what I find on youtube.
